verses.text: Jacob lifts his eyes and sees Esau coming, with the four hundred men. He arranges his family: the handmaids and their children first, Leah and hers behind them, and Rachel with Joseph at the back. He puts the most loved farthest from the front.

Jacob lifts his eyes and sees Esau coming, with the four hundred men. He arranges his family: the handmaids and their children first, Leah and hers behind them, and Rachel with Joseph at the back. He puts the most loved farthest from the front.
Then Jacob himself goes ahead and bows seven times to the ground as he approaches his brother. The supplanter bows like a servant before the one he had robbed of the blessing.
But Esau runs to meet him, throws his arms around him, falls on his neck, and kisses him. And they wept. Twenty years of distance dissolve in one embrace. The dreaded reunion is a homecoming.

Who are these with you? asks Esau. The children whom God has graciously given your servant. One by one the families come forward and bow — the handmaids with their children, Leah with hers, finally Joseph and Rachel. Every face Jacob feared losing is now visible.

Who are these with you? asks Esau. The children whom God has graciously given your servant. One by one the families come forward and bow — the handmaids with their children, Leah with hers, finally Joseph and Rachel. Every face Jacob feared losing is now visible.

Who are these with you? asks Esau. The children whom God has graciously given your servant. One by one the families come forward and bow — the handmaids with their children, Leah with hers, finally Joseph and Rachel. Every face Jacob feared losing is now visible.
And what is all this drove I met? To find grace in your sight, my lord. Esau answers gently: I have enough, my brother — keep what is yours. Esau is no longer the one driven by appetite.
And what is all this drove I met? To find grace in your sight, my lord. Esau answers gently: I have enough, my brother — keep what is yours. Esau is no longer the one driven by appetite.

Jacob insists: please, if I have found grace, receive my gift. For seeing your face is like seeing the face of God, and you have received me. Receive my blessing, for God has dealt graciously with me and I have everything I need. Esau accepts.

Jacob insists: please, if I have found grace, receive my gift. For seeing your face is like seeing the face of God, and you have received me. Receive my blessing, for God has dealt graciously with me and I have everything I need. Esau accepts.

Let us go on together, says Esau. Jacob declines politely: the children are young, the flocks have nursing animals; if we drive them hard they will die. Let my lord go on ahead; I will follow slowly, as the children and animals can. Reconciliation does not have to mean reunion.

Let us go on together, says Esau. Jacob declines politely: the children are young, the flocks have nursing animals; if we drive them hard they will die. Let my lord go on ahead; I will follow slowly, as the children and animals can. Reconciliation does not have to mean reunion.

Let us go on together, says Esau. Jacob declines politely: the children are young, the flocks have nursing animals; if we drive them hard they will die. Let my lord go on ahead; I will follow slowly, as the children and animals can. Reconciliation does not have to mean reunion.
Esau offers to leave some of his men with Jacob. Jacob declines again: it is enough that I have found grace in your sight. The brothers part well, without owing each other anything.

Esau returns the same day to Seir. Jacob journeys to Succoth, builds himself a house there, and makes booths for his cattle — which is how the place gets its name (Succoth means booths). For the first time he is building, not running.

Esau returns the same day to Seir. Jacob journeys to Succoth, builds himself a house there, and makes booths for his cattle — which is how the place gets its name (Succoth means booths). For the first time he is building, not running.

Then he comes to Shechem in Canaan — safely back in the land. He buys a parcel of ground from the sons of Hamor for a hundred pieces of money. And he erects an altar and names it El-elohe-Israel — God, the God of Israel. The new name takes its first stone.
